county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1816 from Monroe County. County seat Montgomery (state capital). ADAH located here. First capital of the Confederacy (1861). Civil rights movement landmarks (Rosa Parks, Dexter Avenue Church, Civil Rights Memorial). Extensive county records due to capital city status.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Montgomery (the state capital). Montgomery County records are intact from 1816 and are among the most extensive in Alabama due to the capital city's importance. ADAH is located in Montgomery and holds both state and county records. The Montgomery County Archives has deed books, probate records, court records, and marriage records. Montgomery was the first capital of the Confederacy (1861). The Rosa Parks Museum, the Dexter Avenue King Memorial Baptist Church, and the Civil Rights Memorial document the civil rights era. For African American research, Montgomery County has extensive slaveholder records, estate inventories, Freedmen's Bureau records, and African American church records. The First White House of the Confederacy has Civil War-era records. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm. The Montgomery Genealogical Society maintains research files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Montgomery County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee Cession as Cotaco County; renamed Morgan 1821. County seat Decatur. Tennessee River port and railroad junction. Battle of Decatur (1864). Wheeler National Wildlife Refuge. One of Alabama's original counties with intact records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Decatur. Morgan County records are intact from 1818 — one of Alabama's original counties. Decatur was a key Tennessee River port and railroad junction. The Battle of Decatur (1864) caused significant Civil War damage to the city but county records survived. The Morgan County Archives in Decatur has excellent holdings. The Wheeler National Wildlife Refuge (est. 1938) covers part of the county. The Old State Bank in Decatur (1833) is a landmark of early Alabama banking. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books, probate, and marriages. The Tennessee Valley Genealogical Society covers this region. Decatur Public Library has local history collections. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Morgan County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1819 from Dallas, Marengo, and Montgomery counties. County seat Marion. Major Black Belt plantation county. Antebellum educational center (Judson College 1838, Howard College 1841). Lincoln Normal School (1868). Coretta Scott King birthplace.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Marion. Perry County records are intact from 1819. Marion was an important antebellum educational center — Judson College (1838, the first Baptist women's college in the US) and Howard College (now Samford University, founded 1841) were both in Marion. Lincoln Normal School (est. 1868) was one of Alabama's earliest African American schools. Perry County was a major Black Belt cotton county with large enslaved populations. The Perry County Historical Society has local materials. Freedmen's Bureau records for Perry County are at NARA. FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records documenting enslaved persons. The Coretta Scott King birthplace is in Perry County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Perry County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.record_losses: 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most records prior to that date
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1820 from Tuscaloosa County. County seat Carrollton. 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most records. Black Belt plantation county with very high enslaved population. Famous 'face in the window' legend at the courthouse.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Carrollton. The 1876 courthouse fire destroyed most records prior to that date — this is a burned county. The famous 'face in the window' of the Pickens County Courthouse (lightning-etched image from 1878) is a local legend. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for pre-1876 microfilm copies. Pickens County was formed in 1820 from Tuscaloosa and was a major Black Belt cotton county. The county had one of the highest percentages of enslaved persons in Alabama. The Reform area was a center of antebellum plantation culture. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records from the 1876 restart. ADAH holds county tax records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1821 from Henry and Montgomery counties. County seat Troy. Troy University (1887). Agricultural county (cotton, peanuts, timber). Parent county of several surrounding counties. Pike Pioneer Museum documents early settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Troy. Pike County records are intact from 1821. Troy University (founded 1887) has local history collections in the Troy University Library. The county was a major agricultural area with cotton, peanuts, and timber. Pike County was the parent of several surrounding counties. The Pike Pioneer Museum in Troy documents early settlement. The county had significant enslaved populations in the plantation areas. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books, probate records, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records. The Pike County Historical Society maintains family files. Federal land patents at BLM GLO document original settlers.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Pike County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat Wedowee. 1897 courthouse fire destroyed many records. Appalachian foothill county with smaller farms. Gold mining before Civil War. Georgia border connections.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Wedowee. The 1897 courthouse fire destroyed many records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for surviving microfilm copies. Randolph County was formed from Creek Cession in 1832. The county is in the Appalachian foothills with smaller farms. Gold mining occurred in the area before the Civil War. The county had mixed loyalties during the Civil War. The Randolph County Historical Society has local materials. The county's proximity to the Georgia line means many families had cross-border connections — check Randolph County and Cleburne County records. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1868 courthouse fire destroyed early records; county seat moved from Crawford
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Creek Cession. County seat Phenix City (since 1935; previously Crawford, then Seale). 1868 courthouse fire destroyed early records. Fort Mitchell was Creek removal assembly point. Phenix City 'cleanup' (1954).
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Phenix City. The 1868 courthouse fire destroyed early records — this is a burned county. The county seat was originally at Crawford, then moved to Seale, and finally to Phenix City in 1935. Russell County was formed from Creek Cession in 1832. Phenix City had a notorious reputation for vice and corruption until the Albert Patterson assassination (1954) led to a National Guard cleanup. Fort Mitchell was a federal military post during Creek removal. The Russell County Historical Commission has local materials. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records. Check Columbus, Georgia records for connected families across the Chattahoochee River.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Russell County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Montgomery County — one of Alabama's original counties. County seat Columbiana. Shelby Iron Works (1846) was critical for Confederacy. Calera limestone quarrying. Now part of Birmingham metro suburban growth.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Columbiana. Shelby County records are intact from 1818 — one of Alabama's original counties. The county was a center of early iron manufacturing; the Shelby Iron Works (1846) was critical for the Confederacy. Calera was a major limestone quarrying town. The county has shifted from rural agriculture to suburban growth as part of the greater Birmingham metro area. The Shelby County Historical Society and Museum in Columbiana has local materials. The University of Montevallo (1896) has regional manuscript collections. FamilySearch has microfilm of deed books, probate, and marriages. ADAH holds county tax records. Old Cahaba was near the Shelby County border.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1818 from Cherokee and Creek Cession — one of Alabama's original counties. Unique dual county seats: Ashville and Pell City. Appalachian foothills and piedmont. Coosa River boundary. Logan Martin Dam.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Ashville (with a second courthouse in Pell City). St. Clair County is unusual in Alabama for having two county seats — Ashville and Pell City. Records from 1818 are relatively intact. The county straddles the Appalachian foothills and the piedmont. The Coosa River forms part of the county boundary. The county had both farming and early mining communities. The St. Clair County Historical Society has local materials. Pell City became important after the construction of Logan Martin Dam (1960s). FamilySearch has microfilm of deeds, probate, and marriages. Check both courthouse locations for records. ADAH holds county tax records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for St. Clair County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Marriage Records -- Probate Judge / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: St. Clair County Probate Judge, Ashville / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and bonds for St. Clair County from 1818; Probate Judge issues marriage licenses in Alabama; FamilySearch has indexed many Alabama marriages

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for St. Clair County; Alabama first enumerated in 1820 federal census; slave schedules in 1850 and 1860; agricultural and manufacturing schedules available

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Tax Records -- ADAH
source_profiles.repository: Alabama Department of Archives and History, Montgomery
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County tax assessment records for St. Clair County listing property owners, acreage, livestock, and enslaved persons (pre-1865); ADAH holds microfilm of Alabama county tax records; essential for tracking family movements and wealth

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Federal Land Patents -- BLM General Land Office
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for St. Clair County from the General Land Office; Alabama is a public land state — original settlers purchased land from the federal government; patents searchable and downloadable at BLM GLO website

source_profiles.source_name: St. Clair County Church and Cemetery Records
source_profiles.repository: ADAH / FamilySearch / Local churches and cemeteries
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Church records (Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian) for St. Clair County; cemetery transcriptions at Find A Grave and USGenWeb; ADAH has some microfilmed church records; essential for vital events before state registration (1908)

county_metadata.record_losses: 1870 courthouse fire; many early records destroyed
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Formed 1832 from Choctaw Cession. County seat Livingston. 1870 courthouse fire destroyed many early records. Wealthy Black Belt plantation county. University of West Alabama in Livingston. Gainesville was an antebellum Tombigbee River port.
county_metadata.research_tips: Probate Judge office in Livingston. The 1870 courthouse fire destroyed many early records — this is a burned county. Check ADAH and FamilySearch for surviving copies. Sumter County was formed from Choctaw Cession in 1832 and was one of the wealthiest Black Belt plantation counties. The University of West Alabama in Livingston has local history collections. The county had massive enslaved populations — estate inventories and slaveholder tax records at ADAH are critical for African American research. Freedmen's Bureau records for Sumter County are at NARA. Gainesville was an important antebellum river port on the Tombigbee. FamilySearch has microfilm of surviving records from 1870 restart.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office federal land patents for Sumter County searchable online; Alabama is a public land state with original federal land grants
